macro_events.event_summary: Oil prices spiked after Iran and U.S. forces attacked commercial ships, escalating tensions over the Strait of Hormuz, a critical chokepoint for ~20% of global oil flows.
primary_drivers: Higher crude price realization for upstream producers
Geopolitical risk premium on tanker day-rates and insurance
Energy security bid for domestic/non-Gulf production

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with significant upstream leverage; each $10/bbl Brent move translates to meaningful EPS upside. Direct beneficiary of higher realized crude prices without Hormuz transit exposure relative to Gulf NOCs.
Upstream-weighted major; Permian and US GoM production benefits from higher prices while Chevron's Hormuz transit risk is limited vs. regional peers.
Pure-play Permian E&P with high oil cut (~65%) and operational leverage; every dollar of Brent flows near-directly to cash flow. Classic geopolitical spike beneficiary.
Product tanker operator — Hormuz disruption reroutes cargoes on longer ton-mile voyages and spikes clean tanker spot rates; insurance war-risk premiums also lift charter economics.
Crude tanker (VLCC/Suezmax) pure-play; Hormuz escalation historically drives VLCC spot rates sharply higher on rerouting and war-risk insurance passthrough.
Cheniere — US LNG exporter benefits as European/Asian buyers pay up for non-Gulf supply; Qatari LNG (~20% of global) transits Hormuz, making US cargoes a security premium substitute.
Defense prime with direct exposure to Middle East munitions demand (PAC-3, THAAD, Javelin restocking) if conflict broadens; historically bid on Iran escalation headlines.
Oilfield services leveraged to North American completion activity; sustained >$85 Brent typically accelerates US shale capex plans, benefiting frac and pressure-pumping pricing.

losers.rationale: Jet fuel is ~25-30% of Delta's operating costs; a sustained crude spike directly compresses margins with limited ability to pass through via fares in the short term.
Same fuel-cost exposure as DAL; United carries meaningful international long-haul capacity making fuel burn per ASM particularly sensitive to crude.
Southwest has historically hedged but fuel remains a top-2 cost line; domestic leisure-heavy network has limited pricing power to offset fuel shocks.
Cruise operator with ~10% opex from bunker fuel; simultaneously faces itinerary risk if Middle East/Red Sea routes require rerouting, double-hit to margins and bookings.
Express air freight network with high jet fuel intensity; fuel surcharges recover costs with a lag and B2B volume softening limits passthrough.
Commodity chemicals producer with naphtha/ethane feedstock exposure; oil-linked feedstock costs rise faster than contract pricing can reset, compressing spreads.
Consumer discretionary pressure from higher gasoline prices acts as a tax on low/middle-income consumers — Walmart's core demographic sees reduced discretionary wallet share.

headlines: Oil prices jump after Iran and U.S. attack commercial ships as tensions escalate over Strait of Hormuz

macro_events.event_summary: AI startups absorbed $242B in Q1 2026 venture funding, exceeding all of 2025, signaling continued massive capital flows into AI infrastructure and model development.
primary_drivers: VC dollars flow downstream to AI compute/infrastructure suppliers (GPUs, networking, power, cooling)
Validates AI capex super-cycle thesis, supporting hyperscaler and semi multiples
Capital concentration in private AI raises competitive/funding pressure on public AI software incumbents and small-cap competitors

beneficiaries.rationale: VC funding into AI startups converts directly into GPU orders; OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, and Mistral-tier raises historically translate to ~50-70% of compute spend going to NVIDIA H100/B200/GB200 systems. Most direct revenue link to private AI capex.
Custom AI ASIC and networking (Tomahawk/Jericho) beneficiary as hyperscalers and large AI labs build out clusters funded partly by these VC rounds; second-largest AI silicon vendor by revenue.
Pure-play GPU cloud (CoreWeave) is a primary destination for VC-funded AI startups that don't want to commit to AWS/Azure long-term contracts; backlog directly tracks AI startup formation.

Vertiv supplies liquid cooling and power distribution for AI data centers; capex pull-through from AI buildouts has driven order book growth. Direct exposure to data center expansion funded by this capital.
Arista provides 400G/800G Ethernet switching for AI back-end networks; tier-2 cloud and neocloud buildouts (often funded by these VC dollars) increasingly favor Arista over InfiniBand.
Nebius is another neocloud / GPU-as-a-service provider directly competing for AI startup workloads; smaller float makes it more sensitive to AI capex headlines than CRWV.
All advanced AI silicon (NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom ASICs) is fabbed at TSMC on N4/N3/CoWoS; sustained AI startup funding extends the leading-edge wafer and advanced-packaging demand cycle.
HBM3e/HBM4 supply is sold out through 2026; every incremental GPU shipment to a VC-funded AI lab pulls Micron HBM content. Tightest supply-demand link in memory.

losers.rationale: Increasingly competing with VC-funded AI-native data platforms (e.g., private LLM/agent infra startups); $242B funding pool intensifies competitive pressure on legacy data warehouse pricing and AI workload share.
RPA incumbent vulnerable to agentic-AI startups absorbing this VC capital (e.g., Adept-style, Sierra, Decagon); core automation TAM under direct attack from better-funded private competitors.
HR/finance SaaS faces emerging AI-native back-office startups well-funded by this capital wave; risk of seat-based pricing model erosion as agents replace users.

headlines: AI Absorbs $242 Billion in Q1 Venture Funding, Exceeding All of 2025 Combined
